questions.question:
The pseudocode of a function fun() is given below:

fun(int A[0, ..., n-1]) {
  for i = 0 to n-2
    for j = 0 to n-i-2
      if (A[j] > A[j+1]) then
        swap A[j] and A[j+1]
}

Let A[0], ..., A[29] be an array storing 30 distinct integers in descending order. The number of swap operations that will be performed, if the function is called with A[0], ..., A[29] as argument, is ________. (Answer in integer)
questions.to_find: Number of swap operations performed by the function on a 30-element descending array

understand.plain: The function implements Bubble Sort. For an array in descending order (worst case), every comparison results in a swap. We need to count the total number of comparisons (which equals total swaps in worst case) made by the nested loops for n=30.

keywords.explain: A sorting algorithm that repeatedly compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are out of order.
keywords.example: For [3,2,1], one pass swaps (3,2) then (3,1), giving [1,2,3] after all passes.

keywords.explain: When the input is sorted in reverse order, every adjacent pair satisfies A[j] > A[j+1], so every comparison triggers a swap.
keywords.example: Array [5,4,3,2,1]: every single comparison leads to a swap.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='140' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='120' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='38' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Loop Iteration Count (n=30)</text><text x='30' y='65' class='sub ink'>i=0: j from 0 to 28 → 29 comparisons (swaps)</text><text x='30' y='85' class='sub ink'>i=1: j from 0 to 27 → 28 comparisons (swaps)</text><text x='30' y='105' class='sub ink'>...  i=28: j from 0 to 0 → 1 comparison (swap)</text><text x='30' y='122' class='tag ink'>Total = 29+28+...+1 = 29×30/2 = 435</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table showing that for i=0 the inner loop runs 29 times, for i=1 it runs 28 times, down to i=28 running 1 time, totaling 435 iterations.

given.aim: Count the total number of swap operations for a 30-element descending array passed to the bubble sort function.

terms.meaning: Every element A[j] > A[j+1], so every comparison triggers a swap
terms.connects: Guarantees worst-case: all comparisons result in swaps

terms.term: Outer loop: i = 0 to n-2, Inner loop: j = 0 to n-i-2
terms.meaning: The nested loop structure of bubble sort
terms.example: For i=0: j runs 0..28 (29 steps); for i=1: j runs 0..27 (28 steps)
terms.connects: Total iterations = sum from k=1 to n-1 of k

given.plan: Since the array is in descending order, every comparison A[j] > A[j+1] is true, so swaps = total comparisons. Count total iterations of the inner loop by summing the inner loop count for each value of i from 0 to n-2.

steps.title: Count inner loop iterations for each i
steps.formula_raw: Inner loop runs from j=0 to n-i-2, giving (n-i-1) iterations
steps.apply: For i=0: n-0-1 = 29 iterations. For i=1: 28. ... For i=28: n-28-1 = 1 iteration.
steps.note: The outer loop runs for i=0,1,...,n-2 = 0,1,...,28, that is 29 values of i.

steps.title: Sum total iterations (= total swaps in worst case)
steps.apply: n=30: Total = (30-1)*30/2 = 29*30/2 = 870/2 = 435. Since every comparison results in a swap (descending array), total swaps = 435.
steps.note: This is the classic worst-case complexity formula for bubble sort: n*(n-1)/2 swaps.

questions.formula_note: Bubble sort worst-case swap count: (n-1)*n/2, achieved when input is in reverse (descending) order.

questions.question:
A priority queue Q is used to implement a stack that stores characters. PUSH(C) is implemented as INSERT(Q, C, K) where K is an appropriate integer key chosen by the implementation. POP is implemented as DELETEMIN. For a sequence of operations, the keys chosen are in

A. non-increasing order
B. non-decreasing order
C. strictly increasing order
D. strictly decreasing order
questions.to_find: The order in which keys must be chosen when using a min-priority queue to simulate a stack

understand.plain: A stack follows LIFO (Last In, First Out). If we use a min-priority queue and DELETEMIN for POP, the most recently pushed element must always have the smallest key so it gets popped first. This means each new PUSH must use a key strictly smaller than the previous one.

keywords.explain: Last element inserted is the first one removed.
keywords.example: Push A, Push B, Push C → Pop gives C, then B, then A.

keywords.explain: Removes and returns the element with the minimum key from the priority queue.
keywords.example: If keys are 5,3,7, DELETEMIN returns the element with key 3.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='140' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='35' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Stack via Min-Priority Queue</text><text x='30' y='60' class='sub ink'>PUSH('A') → INSERT(Q,'A', 100)  key=100</text><text x='30' y='80' class='sub ink'>PUSH('B') → INSERT(Q,'B', 50)   key=50</text><text x='30' y='100' class='sub ink'>PUSH('C') → INSERT(Q,'C', 10)   key=10</text><text x='30' y='120' class='sub ink'>POP → DELETEMIN → returns 'C' (key=10) ✓</text><text x='30' y='140' class='tag ink'>Keys: 100, 50, 10 → strictly decreasing</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Example showing PUSH A with key 100, PUSH B with key 50, PUSH C with key 10. DELETEMIN pops C (smallest key=10), which is the last pushed — correctly simulating a stack.

given.aim: Determine the key assignment order required so that DELETEMIN on a priority queue simulates LIFO (stack) behaviour.

terms.meaning: Each pushed character gets an integer key K chosen by the implementation.
terms.connects: The key K controls which element DELETEMIN will return next.

terms.meaning: POP removes the element with the minimum key.
terms.example: If keys present are {5,2,8}, DELETEMIN removes the one with key 2.
terms.connects: For LIFO, the last-inserted element must always have the current minimum key.

terms.meaning: The most recently pushed element must be the next to be popped.
terms.example: Push X (key=10), Push Y (key=5) → POP must give Y, so key of Y must be less than key of X.
terms.connects: Each successive PUSH must use a strictly smaller key than the previous one.

given.plan: Since DELETEMIN returns the minimum-key element, the last pushed element must always carry the smallest key. Therefore, each new key must be strictly less than the previous key — keys are assigned in strictly decreasing order.

steps.title: Analyse what DELETEMIN returns
steps.formula_raw: DELETEMIN returns element with minimum key
steps.apply: For POP to return the most recently pushed element (LIFO), the most recent PUSH must assign a key smaller than all previously assigned keys.
steps.note: Equal keys could cause ambiguity, so strictly decreasing is required.

steps.title: Determine key order across a sequence of pushes
steps.formula_raw: K_i < K_{i-1} for all i > 1 (strictly decreasing)
steps.apply: First PUSH uses key K_1. Second PUSH uses K_2 < K_1. Third uses K_3 < K_2. And so on. This ensures the most recent element always has the globally minimum key, so DELETEMIN always pops in LIFO order.
steps.note: Non-increasing would allow equal keys, but equal keys violate uniqueness of the minimum and could cause incorrect POP ordering. Strictly decreasing is the correct answer.

solution.result: The keys must be chosen in strictly decreasing order — option D.

questions.formula_note: Simulating a stack with a min-priority queue requires each successive key to be strictly smaller so that DELETEMIN always returns the most recently inserted element.

questions.question:
Let A be a priority queue for maintaining a set of elements. Suppose A is implemented using a max-heap data structure. The operation EXTRACT-MAX(A) extracts and deletes the maximum element from A. The operation INSERT(A, key) inserts a new element in A. The properties of a max-heap are preserved at the end of each of these operations.

When A contains m elements, which one of the following statements about the worst case running time of these two operations is TRUE?

A. Both EXTRACT-MAX(A) and INSERT(A, key) run in O(1).
B. EXTRACT-MAX(A) runs in O(1) whereas INSERT(A, key) runs in O(log m).
C. EXTRACT-MAX(A) runs in O(log m) whereas INSERT(A, key) runs in O(m).
D. EXTRACT-MAX(A) runs in O(log m) whereas INSERT(A, key) runs in O(log m).
questions.answer: D. EXTRACT-MAX(A) runs in O(log m) whereas INSERT(A, key) runs in O(log m).
questions.to_find: Worst-case running times of EXTRACT-MAX and INSERT on a max-heap with m elements

understand.plain: A max-heap is a complete binary tree where every parent is greater than or equal to its children. When we extract the maximum or insert a new element, we need to restore the heap property by 'heapifying' up or down the tree, which takes time proportional to the height of the tree.

keywords.explain: A complete binary tree where each node's value is >= its children's values. The maximum is always at the root.
keywords.example: Tree rooted at 10, children 8 and 7, their children 4,5,3,6 — all parents >= children.

keywords.explain: The operation of restoring heap property after an insertion or deletion by comparing and swapping along a root-to-leaf path.
keywords.example: After inserting a large value at the bottom, we sift it up by swapping with parents until heap property holds.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='160' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='35' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Max-Heap: Height = floor(log_2(m))</text><circle cx='210' cy='65' r='18' class='hi'/><text x='210' y='70' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Max</text><line x1='196' y1='82' x2='165' y2='103' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='224' y1='82' x2='255' y2='103' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><circle cx='160' cy='115' r='15' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='120' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>...</text><circle cx='260' cy='115' r='15' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='120' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>...</text><text x='210' y='150' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Both ops traverse at most O(log m) levels</text><text x='210' y='168' class='tag ink' text-anchor='middle'>EXTRACT-MAX: O(log m)  INSERT: O(log m)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Max-heap tree diagram showing that the height is floor(log_2(m)). Both EXTRACT-MAX and INSERT traverse at most O(log m) levels, giving O(log m) worst-case time for both.

given.aim: Identify correct worst-case time complexities of EXTRACT-MAX and INSERT on a max-heap of m elements.

terms.meaning: A complete binary tree satisfying max-heap property; height = floor(log_2(m)).
terms.connects: Height bounds the number of swaps needed in sift-up/sift-down.

terms.meaning: Remove root (max), replace with last element, then sift-down to restore heap.
terms.example: Remove 10 from root, put last element 3 at root, sift down: 3 vs 8 and 7 → swap with 8.
terms.connects: Sift-down traverses at most height levels → O(log m).

terms.meaning: Add new element at end of heap, then sift-up to restore heap.
terms.example: Insert 15 at the end; compare with parent 7 → swap; compare with 10 → swap; now at root.
terms.connects: Sift-up traverses at most height levels → O(log m).

given.plan: Both EXTRACT-MAX and INSERT require a single traversal from root to leaf (or leaf to root) of a complete binary tree with m nodes. The height of such a tree is floor(log_2(m)) = O(log m), so both operations run in O(log m) worst case.

steps.title: Determine the height of a complete binary tree with m nodes
steps.apply: A complete binary tree with m nodes has height floor(log_2(m)). For example, m=15 → height=3; m=16 → height=4.
steps.note: This is the key property that makes heap operations efficient.

steps.title: Analyse EXTRACT-MAX
steps.formula_raw: Sift-down: at most height = O(log m) swaps
steps.apply: Replace root with the last element and remove it (O(1)), then sift-down: compare with children and swap with larger child, repeating at most height = O(log m) times.
steps.note: Worst case: the replacement element is the smallest, so it sifts all the way to a leaf.

steps.title: Analyse INSERT
steps.formula_raw: Sift-up: at most height = O(log m) swaps
steps.apply: Place new element at the next available position (O(1)), then sift-up: compare with parent and swap if larger, repeating at most height = O(log m) times.
steps.note: Worst case: the new element is the largest, so it sifts all the way to the root.

solution.result: Both EXTRACT-MAX(A) and INSERT(A, key) run in O(log m) worst case — option D.

questions.formula_note: Max-heap operations (EXTRACT-MAX, INSERT) both take O(log m) time because the height of a complete binary tree with m nodes is floor(log_2(m)).

questions.question:
Suggest a data structure for representing a subset S of integers from 1 to n. Following operations on the set S are to be performed in constant time (independent of cardinality of S):

i.   MEMBER(X): Check whether X is in the set S or not
ii.  FIND-ONE(S): If S is not empty, return one element of S (any arbitrary element will do)
iii. ADD(X): Add integer X to set S
iv.  DELETE(S, X): Delete integer X from set S

Give pictorial examples of your data structure. Give routines in an English like language. You may assume the data structure has been suitably initialized. Clearly state your assumptions regarding initialization.
questions.answer: A. Boolean array (bit array) of size n
questions.to_find: A data structure that supports MEMBER, FIND-ONE, ADD, DELETE all in O(1) time for a subset of integers 1 to n

understand.plain: We need a data structure for a subset of integers in range [1..n] that supports four operations, all in O(1) time. A simple boolean/bit array of size n (indexed 1 to n) achieves this: arr[x]=true means x is in S, arr[x]=false means x is not. All four operations are direct array accesses.

keywords.explain: An array of size n where arr[i] = 1 (true) if integer i is in the set, and arr[i] = 0 (false) otherwise.
keywords.example: For n=5, if S={2,4}, then arr=[0,1,0,1,0] (1-indexed).

keywords.explain: An operation whose running time does not depend on the number of elements currently in the set.
keywords.example: Array indexing arr[x] is always O(1) regardless of how large S is.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='400' height='150' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='210' y='35' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Boolean Array for S ⊆ {1..7}, S={2,4,6}</text><rect x='30' y='50' width='50' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='63' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>idx</text><text x='55' y='80' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>1 2 3 4 5 6 7</text><rect x='30' y='90' width='50' height='35' class='bg'/><text x='55' y='103' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>val</text><rect x='80' y='90' width='50' height='35' class='no'/><rect x='130' y='90' width='50' height='35' class='ok'/><rect x='180' y='90' width='50' height='35' class='no'/><rect x='230' y='90' width='50' height='35' class='ok'/><rect x='280' y='90' width='50' height='35' class='no'/><rect x='330' y='90' width='50' height='35' class='ok'/><rect x='380' y='90' width='30' height='35' class='no'/><text x='105' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:white'>0</text><text x='155' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:white'>1</text><text x='205' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:white'>0</text><text x='255' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:white'>1</text><text x='305' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:white'>0</text><text x='355' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:white'>1</text><text x='395' y='113' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle' style='fill:white'>0</text><text x='210' y='145' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>MEMBER(4)→arr[4]=1→Yes | ADD(3)→arr[3]=1 | DELETE(S,2)→arr[2]=0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Boolean array of size 7 for S={2,4,6}. Indices 2,4,6 have value 1 (green), others have value 0 (red). Each operation is a direct O(1) array access.

given.aim: Find a data structure for subsets of {1,...,n} supporting MEMBER, FIND-ONE, ADD, DELETE all in O(1).

terms.meaning: Check if X belongs to S.
terms.example: If arr[X]=1, X is in S; otherwise not.

terms.meaning: Return any element currently in S, or report empty.
terms.example: Maintain a variable 'any' pointing to one member; update on ADD/DELETE.
terms.connects: Can be supported in O(1) with an auxiliary pointer or linked structure.

terms.meaning: Insert or remove integer X from S.
terms.example: ADD(5): set arr[5]=1. DELETE(S,3): set arr[3]=0.
terms.connects: Both are direct array writes: O(1).

given.plan: Use a boolean array arr[1..n] initialized to all 0. MEMBER, ADD, and DELETE are single array reads/writes (O(1)). For FIND-ONE, maintain a doubly-linked list of elements currently in S alongside the array; the list head gives one element in O(1), and ADD/DELETE update both the array and the list in O(1).

steps.title: Choose the data structure: boolean array of size n
steps.formula_raw: arr[1..n], arr[i] = 1 iff i in S, else 0
steps.apply: Allocate arr[1..n] initialized to all 0 (empty set). Each index directly corresponds to one integer in {1..n}.
steps.note: Initialization requires O(n) time once, but all subsequent operations are O(1).

steps.title: Implement the four operations
steps.formula_raw: MEMBER(X): return arr[X]; ADD(X): arr[X]=1; DELETE(X): arr[X]=0
steps.apply: MEMBER(X): return arr[X] == 1. ADD(X): arr[X] = 1. DELETE(S,X): arr[X] = 0. FIND-ONE(S): maintain an auxiliary doubly-linked list of all elements currently in S; FIND-ONE returns the head of this list (O(1)); ADD appends to list; DELETE removes from list using a back-pointer stored alongside arr[X].
steps.note: All four operations run in O(1) time (constant, independent of |S|). The auxiliary linked list for FIND-ONE requires O(n) extra space but keeps all operations O(1).

solution.result: A boolean array arr[1..n] (with an optional auxiliary linked list for FIND-ONE) supports all four operations in O(1) time.

questions.formula_note: Boolean/bit array indexed by integer value enables O(1) set membership, insertion, and deletion for integer universes; FIND-ONE is O(1) with an auxiliary linked list of present elements.
